turns.text: Hello Carey, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s funny—I’ve spent so much of my professional life on the other side of these conversations, helping others navigate their own journeys. But sitting here now, at thirty-four weeks, I feel strangely exposed. It’s a bit of a paradox, isn't it? I have all this knowledge, all this clinical vocabulary, and yet it feels like it’s becoming a burden rather than a comfort.

My OB is wonderful; she doesn’t treat me like "just another patient" or ignore my background, which I’m so grateful for. But there are days, especially when I’m staring at my chart, where I wish I could just turn my brain off. I look at those acronyms and my mind immediately jumps to the worst-case scenario. At forty, I’m constantly wondering if my body is going to hold up the way it did when I was younger. It’s exhausting to balance that clinical hyper-awareness with the actual, physical reality of carrying this baby.

I keep trying to shift my focus back to the nursery, to the quiet, golden light in the afternoons here in South Hadley, and to the fact that I’ve had a textbook-perfect pregnancy so far. I’m just… I’m really hoping to move away from the "medium risk" label and toward just being a mother. Does that make sense? It’s a lot to hold.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

To be honest, when I saw that positive test, my first thought wasn't "Oh, how magical." My brain immediately pivoted to risk assessment. I expected to be treated like a liability—a "geriatric pregnancy" in the sterile, cold sense of the term. I anticipated needing to fight to be heard, to push back against a medical system that often views women my age as a checklist of potential complications rather than people. I expected lots of talk about genetic screening, blood pressure, and induction windows.

In reality, the environment was… kinder than I’d braced myself for. My OB office feels less like a sterile lab and more like a space where they actually prioritize the person. The staff is warm, and because of my professional background, I’m treated with a professional transparency that I truly value. We talk through the data, and she listens when I voice my concerns about the "medium risk" label hanging over me. 

Still, there’s an underlying tension I couldn't escape. Even in a supportive office, the clinical environment is fundamentally designed to watch for failure. Every time I get on the scale or have my blood pressure taken, I’m waiting for the "off" signal. It’s hard to switch off the part of me that is always scanning for a problem, even when the care is genuinely good.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise, and it’s a double-edged sword, was just how much autonomy I was allowed to have because of my background. I walked in expecting to be managed, but my OB essentially looked at me and said, "We’re going to partner on this."

On one hand, it was incredibly empowering. I wasn't just being talked *at*; I was being consulted as a peer. That kind of transparency is rare, and it made me feel seen, not just as a medical file but as a woman who understands the stakes. 

But, honestly? It was also terrifying. It stripped away that protective layer of "the doctor knows best and I’m just along for the ride." When you're the one who understands the clinical pathways, you don't get the luxury of blind faith. When my OB explains the "why" behind every test or recommendation, I’m mentally running the differential diagnoses right along with her. It confirmed that there’s a cost to knowing too much. I think I realized then that I wasn't going to get to be the "easy" patient. I had to be an active participant in my own monitoring, which kept me in that headspace of constantly assessing my own mortality and the baby's safety rather than just settling into the quiet joy of it all. It’s a lonely place to be.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected a very structured, somewhat detached flow of information. You know how it goes: the technician stays quiet during the scan, you wait for the radiologist’s report to hit the portal, and then you get a succinct phone call from a nurse or the OB summarizing the findings. I expected to be the one translating the jargon for myself, braced for the clinical brevity that we so often rely on to stay efficient.

The reality was surprisingly human, but that actually added to my anxiety in a weird way. My doctor is so communicative that she talks through the ultrasound in real-time, explaining what she’s seeing, the blood flow, the markers. In theory, that’s the "gold standard" of care—complete transparency. But being a professional, I know that even a clear scan is just a snapshot in time. Every time she points to a healthy measurement, my brain is already calculating the margins of error. 

When she gives me the "all clear," I feel relieved for about ten minutes, and then I’m back to wondering what might show up at the next milestone. There’s no "closing the file" when you’re this informed. You don’t get to just put the results in a drawer and go have lunch. You carry that data around, weighing it against the "what-ifs," and it makes every scan feel like a high-stakes exam I’m taking, not just a window into my baby’s world.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

That is a really astute question. It’s not so much that there’s a gap in the *amount* of information; if anything, there’s an information surplus. The gap is in the emotional capacity to process that information as a mother rather than a clinician. 

In my professional life, I’ve always operated on the premise that more data equals more control. If I have the stats, I can mitigate the risk. But in this pregnancy, the data doesn’t actually grant me control—it just grants me awareness of my own helplessness. When I’m given a clean bill of health, part of me feels a deep, logical satisfaction. But another part, the part that keeps me up at night, feels like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop because I know exactly how often the "textbook" results are superseded by an unexpected complication.

I don’t need more leaflets or clinical explanations. What I find myself craving—and what feels impossible to ask for—is for someone to hold the space for my fear without pathologizing it. I want to be able to say, "I’m scared," and have it met with a human reassurance that goes beyond, "But your labs look fine." The data is there, but the reassurance is hard to find, even when I’m the one reading the charts.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I suppose I went in with a bit of a cynical, pragmatic view. Having worked in the system, I knew the reality of modern practice: "continuity of care" often sounds better in a brochure than it actually plays out in a busy clinic. I fully expected to be shuffled between residents, fellows, and nurses, spending half my time repeating my history to someone who hadn’t glanced at my chart since their morning coffee. I was braced for that fragmented experience where you’re just another chart number in a rotating door.

To my surprise, the practice I’m with has been remarkably consistent. I see my primary OB at almost every visit. She knows me, she knows my background, and she doesn't treat me like a stranger. There’s a genuine continuity there that I wasn't sure existed anymore. 

Yet, even with that consistency, there’s a persistent loneliness. My OB provides excellent *clinical* continuity, but I’m not sure anyone is providing *emotional* continuity. Because she respects my intelligence, she treats me as a colleague-in-the-room, which is fine for reviewing data, but it prevents the kind of soft, maternal-leaning support I realize I’m actually starving for. I have the same face to look at, which is a gift, but I’m still the one holding the bulk of this anxiety by myself.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Even with the consistency of my primary OB, there have been a few appointments where I’ve seen a nurse practitioner or a colleague to cover her, and it's jarring. It creates this immediate, reflexive need to "perform." I feel this pressure to present myself as the "model patient"—composed, informed, and definitely not the emotional, vulnerable forty-year-old who worries she’s reaching the end of her capacity.

When I’m with a provider who doesn’t know my history, I find myself over-explaining. I’ll rattle off my recent vitals or interpret a scan result before they’ve even had a chance to say hello, just to establish that I’m "in the club." It’s a defense mechanism. If I sound like I know what I’m talking about, maybe they’ll forget to treat me like a high-risk outlier. 

Practically, it just adds to the mental fatigue. You spend the first ten minutes of an appointment establishing rapport or "proving" your competency, which leaves less time for the real stuff—the actual, human experience of being pregnant. It turns these appointments into a series of briefings rather than a relationship. Even if they are perfectly capable, those fragmented visits leave me feeling drained. It’s like being a performer who has to constantly reset the stage for a new audience, never really being allowed to just collapse into the chair and be the one who needs help.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected a clinical assessment—measurements, fundal height, the standard checking of boxes. In my head, I figured it would be handled with a professional, detached efficiency. I suppose I wanted it to be just that: professional. I wanted to be a body that was being cared for, not a body that was being scrutinized as a "geriatric" vessel.

When it actually happens, the experience is so much more layered. It feels… invasive, in a way I didn't anticipate. My OB is gentle, but there’s this constant clinical focus on the *limitations* of my body because of my age. When she’s feeling the baby’s position or checking my vitals, I feel like she’s hunting for signs of wear and tear. Is the placenta functioning? Are the vessels clear? It’s not just a check-up; it’s an investigation into whether my body is "failing" to keep up with the demands of the pregnancy.

It’s humiliating, honestly. I feel like a classic car being inspected for rust before a long drive. I know the intentions are good—it’s all about safety—but it’s difficult to reconcile that cold, diagnostic touch with the fact that I’m trying to grow a person. I want to feel celebrated, or at least accepted, but I feel like I’m constantly being measured against a standard I’m failing to meet by virtue of my birth year.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Fortunately, I haven't had to navigate a formal diagnosis like pre-eclampsia—my vitals are holding steady—but because of my age, I live in a constant state of "pre-emptive management." I expected that if something were to show up, it would be a clear, actionable problem: you get the diagnosis, you follow the protocol, and you manage the outcome. I’m a professional; I like protocols. I find comfort in a list of tasks.

The reality, however, is a persistent, low-level dread that’s much harder to manage. It’s the "what if" that occupies the space where a specific diagnosis hasn't yet landed. I find myself self-policing in ways I didn't expect. I analyze my swelling, my energy levels, and my baby’s movement patterns with a level of scrutiny that would make a medical resident blush. It’s like being a doctor on call for my own body, twenty-four hours a day.

There is no "off" switch. Even when I’m perfectly healthy, I’m mentally managing the *possibility* of illness. It’s exhausting to live in that middle ground, waiting for a label that might never come, but feeling like I’m already living under the weight of it. It’s not the diagnosis that’s the hardest part—it’s the hyper-vigilance that fills the quiet moments.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

I think what’s missing is a space where the "expert" persona I’ve built can be set down. Everyone—my OB, my colleagues, even my friends—sees me as someone who knows the system, so they assume I’m self-sufficient. They give me the technical details, the data, and the clinical roadmaps, but they rarely ask, "How are you *carrying* this?" 

There’s a huge gap in emotional acknowledgement. I’m treated as a peer who can handle the hard truths, but I’m still a pregnant woman who is terrified of the unknown. I have no outlet to say, "I’m scared that my body is going to let me down" without it being met with a clinical reassurance like, "Well, your blood pressure is currently 120/80, so there's no evidence of that." That logic is helpful, but it’s not *comforting*. 

I’m starving for someone to just sit with the fear, to let me be fragile for a moment without trying to "fix" it with statistics. I don’t need more information; I have plenty of that. I need permission to not be the professional, to not be the high-risk patient, and to just be a person who is doing something incredibly brave and really, really hard. The system is built for management, but it feels completely unequipped for the weight of the human experience behind the chart.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

Because of my background, I expected to be the "priority" patient. I’ve seen how the system works, and I knew that if I picked up the phone, I could sound like someone who knows how to navigate the bureaucratic red tape. I expected that if I called with a clinical concern—a change in fetal movement or a spike in blood pressure—I’d get a prompt, efficient response. 

And, for the most part, the system *is* responsive, but it’s a specific kind of responsiveness that feels hollow. When I call, I get quick answers. I get triaged. I get directed to the right tests. But that efficiency is a double-edged sword. It’s almost *too* effective at keeping me in that clinical loop. If I call because I’m anxious, the system responds by offering me a non-stress test or a quick scan. They solve the clinical "problem" of my anxiety by giving me more data. 

But sometimes, when I’m calling, I’m not asking for a test. I’m asking for a lifeline. I’m asking someone to tell me it’s going to be okay, not just that my numbers are stable. The responsiveness is there on a procedural level, but it’s completely disconnected from the human need for reassurance. I feel like I’m accessing the medical machinery perfectly, but I’m still not feeling heard.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Oh, absolutely. And it’s ironic, because I know exactly how dangerous that kind of hesitation can be. I’ve probably lectured patients about not waiting, about "erring on the side of caution." But when it’s your own body, and you’re forty, there’s this crushing fear of being labeled "that patient"—the one who’s overly anxious, the one who’s "high maintenance" because she knows the terminology and is constantly checking the pulse of her own pregnancy.

There was a Tuesday about two weeks ago where I felt a slight shift in the baby’s activity. A normal person might have felt worried, but as someone in the field, I immediately went to the worst-case, statistically significant outcomes. I sat on the edge of my bed for three hours, terrified to call the office. I didn't want to hear the triage nurse sigh, or to have my doctor look at me during the next visit with that "Oh, Carey is spiraling again" expression. I felt this intense pressure to self-regulate because I didn't want to be the "problem" in their schedule. 

I eventually talked myself down by checking my own vitals and watching the clock, but that silence was heavy. I chose to isolate myself in that fear rather than risk being judged by the very system I respect. That’s the most frustrating part—the system doesn't *want* you to feel that way, but the culture of efficiency makes you feel like an intrusion.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I expected a lot of "the village" mentality, especially since this is my second child at forty. I thought people would be more protective, more inclined to handle the heavy lifting, or at least be a steady presence to lean on. I think I romanticized the idea of a community rallying around a "geriatric" pregnancy, maybe hoping for a bit more grace and patience because of the physical toll.

The reality has been much more… fragmented. Because I’m a professional and I’ve been so capable my whole life, people tend to forget that I’m struggling. They see me as "Carey, who knows how this works," so they assume I’m handling it perfectly. My husband is an absolute rock, and we’re in this together, but even he sometimes falls into the trap of looking at the clinical outcomes instead of the emotional reality. He asks about the appointments and the lab results because that’s the language we’ve learned to speak to feel safe.

Friends, though? They’re supportive in a very external way—baby gifts, well-wishes—but there’s a gap in the deeper, more vulnerable check-ins. I feel like I’m constantly performing "I’m fine" to keep from alarming anyone. I’m the one who’s supposed to have the answers, not the one who needs to be held. It’s lonely, feeling like you have to be the expert for everyone else while you’re quietly terrified for yourself.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think the most surprising form of support came from an unexpected place: other moms who are older. I hadn’t really sought out a "community" because I assumed my clinical training was my shield, but finding a few women who have walked this path at forty was a revelation. They don’t want to talk about my blood pressure or my risk factors—they want to talk about the sheer, overwhelming exhaustion of doing this when you’re already established, the feeling of your body feeling "different" than it did twenty years ago. That peer-level validation has been incredibly grounding.

On the flip side, the support I expected—the kind of "soft" care from my inner circle—didn't quite materialize in the way I needed. I think because I’m so articulate about my health and I can explain my risks in such technical detail, my friends and family often assume I have it handled. They hear me list off my prenatal stats and think, "Oh, Carey’s on top of it," so they don't feel the need to check in on my actual heart. It’s like my competence is a wall they can’t see over. I expected people to notice when I looked tired or overwhelmed, but instead, they just see a woman who has a plan. It’s a lonely paradox: the more capable I sound, the more invisible my real needs become.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I’m still in the thick of preparing for that moment, and honestly, the "birth plan" feels like a bit of a farce. I’ve read all the literature, I’ve seen the clinical outcomes, and I know how quickly things can pivot. I expected to be the patient who brought a typed, laminated document detailing my preferences for lighting, music, and the timing of interventions, hoping that a checklist would protect me from the unpredictability of a clinical setting. 

But now? I find myself hesitating to even write one. I worry that if I lay out my "perfect" birth, I’m just setting myself up for a deeper sense of failure if the medical reality requires us to deviate. I’m already carrying this "medium risk" label—what if my plan is just seen as an obstacle to the staff’s clinical protocols? 

I haven’t finalized it yet because I’m paralyzed by the fear that it’s more for me than it is for them. I want to feel like I have a say, like I’m the director of my own experience, but I know all too well that in that room, the "expert" voice—the one with the stethoscope—is the one that holds the power. I’m trying to negotiate between my desire for a gentle, peaceful entrance for this baby and the cold, clinical reality of what could go wrong. It’s a delicate, uncomfortable dance.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

I haven't actually crossed that threshold into labor yet—I’m still at thirty-four weeks, counting down the days—but the *anticipation* of that moment is a weight I carry every single day. I’ve spent my career seeing plans discarded in the blink of an eye when a fetal heart rate drops or a blood pressure reading spikes. I’ve seen that transition from "planned, gentle, autonomous birth" to "emergency, high-intervention protocol" happen in seconds.

Knowing that makes it almost impossible to write a plan that feels authentic. How do I write down "I want peace and quiet" when I also know the clinical markers that would necessitate immediate, loud, and chaotic intervention? It feels like writing a contract that you know the other party has the power to void at any moment. 

My biggest fear isn't just the medical intervention—I respect the necessity of it—it’s the *loss of voice* that accompanies that transition. I’m afraid that if things go sideways, I’ll be reduced to a patient who needs to be managed, rather than a person who needs to be brought along on the decision-making journey. The thought of that transition—from the one who understands the protocol to the one who is subject to it—is probably the most terrifying part of this entire experience.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

Since I’m still navigating the home stretch of these last weeks, I haven't reached the point of labor yet. But the conversations I’ve had with my OB about pain management are already loaded with the baggage of my background. 

I expected, based on my clinical knowledge, to have a spectrum of options presented to me like a menu—epidurals, nitrous oxide, IV meds, natural techniques. I assumed that because I’m so articulate and "in the know," the discussion would be a sophisticated, collaborative negotiation. I expected to be treated as a partner in managing my own physical experience, with my preferences respected as the primary directive.

The reality, however, is that I feel an immense, unspoken pressure to be the "strong" patient. Even when my doctor brings up pain relief, I find myself downplaying my own threshold for discomfort. I feel like if I ask for an epidural too early, or if I struggle with the pain, I’m somehow validating the "medium risk" bias that I’m trying to ignore. It’s like I have to prove that I can handle it, that I’m not just another "anxious" patient who needs to be numbed out. I’m terrified that the moment I lose control of the pain, I’ll lose control of the room. It’s an exhausting, internal negotiation I’m having with myself before I’ve even walked through the hospital doors.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

It’s this strange, internal wall I’ve built. I haven’t even reached the hospital yet, but I can already feel the barriers, and most of them are self-imposed. I know exactly what’s available on the maternity ward—the epidural, the nitrous, the standard pharmacological interventions. But the barrier isn’t a lack of access; it’s the fear of what choosing those things says about me.

Because I’m forty and labeled "medium risk," I feel this irrational, heavy pressure to have a "textbook" birth. In my head, I’ve convinced myself that if I require heavy pain management, it’s just another piece of evidence that my body is struggling, that I’m not the resilient, capable woman I’m trying to project. I worry that if I ask for pain relief, the providers will view it as a clinical red flag rather than a human need. 

I know, logically, that this is ridiculous—that I’m entitled to whatever comfort I need—but the fear of being seen as "fragile" or "high-risk" is so strong that I feel like I might talk myself out of the help I actually need. I’m stuck in this loop where I’m afraid that by admitting I’m in pain, I’m admitting defeat. It’s not the system keeping options from me; it’s my own desperate need to appear in control that’s blocking my own access to comfort.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I’m still thirty-four weeks, so I haven’t walked through those hospital doors for the main event yet. But I spend a lot of time imagining it, and frankly, it feels like bracing for a collision.

I keep picturing myself walking into the unit and feeling that familiar, clinical hum—the beeping monitors, the staff rushing between rooms, the sterile air. I expect to feel like a "case" that needs to be processed. I have this desperate hope that I’ll be welcomed as a person, not a high-risk statistic, but I’m terrified that the moment I check in, the professional side of me will be ignored, and I'll be treated like someone who doesn't understand what’s happening to her.

I’m worried that the environment itself—the fluorescent lights, the cold metal, the sheer *efficiency* of the labor ward—is going to strip away the last bits of my calm. I want the room to feel like a sanctuary, but I know the reality is that it's a high-functioning medical workspace. Every time I think about arriving, I feel this tightening in my chest. I’m scared that the environment will force me into that "patient" box so hard that I won’t be able to breathe, let alone labor peacefully. I’m just trying to figure out how to walk in there and stay "Carey," not just "Patient 40-year-old-nullipara-ish-wait-multipara-at-forty." It feels impossible to reconcile the clinical space with the soft, quiet transition I want for my baby.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

Even though I haven't arrived for the birth yet, I’ve walked through those labor and delivery halls enough times in my career to know exactly what’s waiting for me. The biggest, most jarring surprise—every single time—is the disconnect between the clinical intensity and the life-altering moment taking place. 

I’m bracing for that sensory overload: the hum of the fetal monitors, the bright, unforgiving LED lights, and that specific, antiseptic smell that somehow feels colder in a maternity ward than anywhere else. I know that the moment I walk in, the atmosphere is going to prioritize *monitoring* over *being*. It’s a space designed for the "what ifs."

The irony that really keeps me up at night is that I’m the one who designed my own anxiety. I know that the physical layout is meant to keep me and the baby safe, but it feels like the architecture of surveillance. I’m preparing myself for the shock of it being so… bureaucratic. I’m terrified that the moment I walk through those doors, I won’t be a woman bringing a new life into the world, but a body being cataloged by the machinery. It’s that transition—from the quiet, golden light of my home nursery to the harsh, bright efficiency of the clinical setting—that I don’t think I’m ready for. It feels like walking into a storm I’ve spent my life studying, but one I’ve never actually had to survive myself.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I haven't gone into labor yet, but I’ve spent countless hours agonizing over this exact scenario. Because of my clinical background, I have this very specific, idealistic expectation: if things go south, I expect a "shared decision-making" model. I want my OB to look at me, tell me the data in plain language, explain the necessity of the intervention, and give me a moment to process the pivot before we act. I want to feel like a partner, not an object being acted upon.

But then I recall the reality of those high-pressure moments—the ones I’ve been involved in myself. When the fetal heart rate drops or the monitor goes haywire, the room changes instantly. The conversation goes from "let's discuss" to "we need to do this now." The grace of a calm conversation disappears, replaced by the necessity of speed. 

I’m terrified that when that moment comes, my training won't save me; it’ll just make me more aware of what’s failing. I fear the transition from being a sentient person with a voice to being an emergency that needs to be "resolved." I’m worried that the "shared decision-making" I crave will be replaced by a frantic command, and that I’ll be left feeling like I wasn’t even in the room for the most important decision of my life. That loss of agency is what haunts me.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

Because I haven't reached the birth yet, I’m still living in this limbo between anticipation and dread. I keep playing out these scenarios in my head, and I find myself expecting something that feels impossible to quantify. I want the doctor or midwife to act as a *translator*—someone who sees that I’m drowning in my own clinical knowledge and reaches out to pull me back into the human experience of birth. I expect them to see past the chart, past the "medium risk" label, and see the woman who is actually holding the baby. 

I’m terrified that the reality will be a sterile, transactional form of care. I’ve seen the clinical side of it: you're there to manage the birth, not necessarily to hold the person’s hand through the existential weight of it. I fear that the moment labor intensifies, the provider will pivot entirely to the vitals, the progression, the "clinical outcomes." 

I’m so worried that when I need a moment of grace, I’ll get a clinical assessment instead. I want them to say, "I know you’re smart, I know you’re a professional, but right now, you’re just a mother, and you’re doing great." I’m bracing myself for the cold efficiency that defines our field, even though I’m desperate for something much warmer. It’s hard to ask for that when you’re already supposed to be the "expert" in the room.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

Since I haven't reached that threshold yet, I find myself spending my nights in South Hadley rehearsing this exact, fragile moment. In my head, I’ve painted a picture of absolute, quiet grace: the baby placed immediately on my chest, the room fading into a background hum, that primal surge of relief and connection. I expect the staff to step back, to give us that sacred, uninterrupted time to just *exist* together before the standard assessments take over. I expect a transition that honors the humanity of what just happened.

But because I know the clinical protocols, I’m haunted by the "what-ifs." I’m bracing for the reality where there might be a complication, or where the "medium-risk" label means the neonatology team needs to intervene right away. I’m scared of the disruption—the cords, the sensors, the separation. I’m terrified that those first moments won't be a quiet bonding experience, but a frantic clinical hand-off. 

I’m currently caught between two versions of this: the maternal dream of peace and the professional knowledge of the potential for crisis. It’s hard to let myself fully lean into the dream when I know how quickly the room can fill with the sound of monitors and hurried voices. I’m just trying to prepare for the possibility that I might have to fight for that time, and the exhaustion of having to advocate for "peace" in a place designed for "emergency."
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

I haven’t walked through those doors yet, but the *absence*—the thing I fear won’t happen—is that sense of true, unhurried stillness. 

In my professional life, I’ve seen the "Golden Hour" managed with a stopwatch. It’s a protocol. You deliver, you do the skin-to-skin, you check the clock, you start the vitals. It’s efficient, it’s safe, and it’s entirely clinical. But what I fear is that there will be no space for the *witnessing* of the mother. I’m terrified that I’ll be lying there, having just moved mountains, and the staff will be so focused on the placenta or the APGAR score or the next chart entry that they’ll treat me like a piece of equipment that just finished its cycle.

I stay up at night wondering if I’ll have to be the one to remind them, "Wait—just look at me for a second." I want to be held in that moment of transition, not just managed. It stays with me because I’m so afraid that I’ll have to fight to be human, and that the fight itself will ruin the peace I’ve been trying to cultivate for nine months. I don't want to be a patient who checked all the boxes; I want to be a woman who was finally allowed to be still.

You know, it’s strange. I’m sitting here in this quiet nursery—the wallpaper is half-finished, the crib is waiting—and I keep looking at my hands. They’re the hands of a professional, someone who knows exactly how to handle a complication, who knows the trajectory of a labor gone wrong. But they feel so small right now.

I think the biggest thing I’m grappling with is this pervasive sense of being a "double agent." I’m trying to be the expectant mother who trusts the miracle of it all, but I’m simultaneously the clinician who is constantly scanning for the glitch in the system. It’s an exhausting way to exist. I’m so worried that by knowing the "how" and the "why" of every medical possibility, I’ve robbed myself of the ability to just *surrender* to the process. 

I’m really hoping that when the time comes—when the contractions start and the clinical reality takes over—I can actually set that part of my brain aside. I’m hoping that I’ll be able to tell my OB, "I know what you're doing, but please, just tell me I’m okay." I want to be held by the care I’ve chosen, rather than just being a partner in the data collection. Does that make sense? I’m just… I’m tired of being the one who knows what’s coming. I want to be surprised by the joy, not the diagnosis.

It’s funny, the more we talk, the more I realize how much I’ve been guarding myself. I’ve spent months performing this role of the "informed colleague," partially because it’s how I’ve been socialized, and partially because it’s a armor. If I’m the one who understands the clinical pathways, I feel like I’m less likely to be blindsided. But I’m starting to see that this armor is actually quite heavy. It’s kept me from ever really being *held* by the system I’m part of.

There’s a specific kind of grief in that, I think—the grief of knowing I might not get that "pure" experience of just being a mother because I’m forever calculating the risks. I wonder if, after this baby arrives, I’ll be able to look back at my chart and see anything other than a sequence of events to be managed. 

I’m so deeply invested in the outcome—obviously, it’s my child—but I’m also realizing I’m invested in the *story* of the birth itself. I want the story to be one of strength and grace, not just a series of successful clinical interventions. I want to look back at the room in South Hadley—or rather, the hospital room—and remember the way my husband looked at me, or the first time I held this little life, rather than the monitor readings or the timing of the pitocin. I’m just trying to figure out how to be the person who lives the story, rather than the one who’s busy editing the clinical notes in her head while it’s happening. Is that too much to ask for, even when you know how the machinery works?

I think the core of it—the thing that’s really keeping me up these last few nights—is the fear that if I don’t manage this perfectly, I’ve somehow failed both the clinician in me and the mother in me. I keep thinking about the "medium risk" label. It’s just two words on a screen, but to me, it feels like a prophecy. 

When I look at the baby’s clothes neatly folded in the drawers here in the nursery, I try to imagine who is going to be in them. Will they be a child who grew from a pregnancy that was a triumph of the system? Or one that survived a series of near-misses? It’s a ridiculous way to frame it, I know, but that’s the professional burden—you don't just see the baby, you see the physiological risks that *could* have been.

I just hope that when the time comes, I’m able to surrender the "doctor" side of my brain to someone else. I really, truly hope that my OB sees me not as a consultant in the room, but as a person who is incredibly vulnerable. If I could give one piece of advice to those researchers you work with, it’s that being "informed" isn't a superpower. It’s a weight. And sometimes, the most clinical-looking person in the room is the one who needs the most permission to just be afraid. 

I’m trying to practice that now. Just being still. Breathing. It’s a work in progress, but maybe that’s all this is—just trying to get to the other side of the charts and into the life that’s waiting for me.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, the "weight" conversation. It’s like a rite of passage for every pregnant woman, but it hits differently when you’re forty. Even with my medical background, I haven't been immune to those subtle, loaded comments—the glances at the chart followed by a "let’s watch the weight gain," or the well-meaning but stinging remarks from acquaintances about how I’m "carrying small" or "not showing much" for my age.

When those comments happen, I feel this immediate, defensive surge of anger. I know the clinical guidelines for BMI and gestational weight gain, and I know exactly what my trajectory looks like compared to the population mean. So when someone—even a provider—mentions it as if I haven't already scrutinized every single pound, I feel belittled. It turns my body into a project that’s failing to meet a benchmark.

It lands like a judgment on my capability. It feels like they’re saying, "Your body is already working overtime because of your age; don’t add this to the list of things you’re messing up." It’s infuriating. I want to tell them that my body is doing the work of growing a human, which is a miracle in itself, regardless of the scale. But instead, I just nod, smile, and swallow the irritation, reinforcing that "good patient" facade while feeling completely disrespected. It’s exhausting to have your physical integrity constantly audited.

I find myself thinking about how much of this performance—this "good, informed patient" routine—is actually a way to protect my own heart. If I can prove that I’ve managed my own care, if I can show them that I’ve read the literature and kept my vitals in check, then maybe, just maybe, I can prevent the thing I fear most: the loss of control. 

But I’m also realizing that this hyper-vigilance, this constant internal monitoring, is a barrier to the very thing I need: connection. I’m so busy managing the clinical experience that I’m not really present for the emotional one. I’m worried that when I look back on this time, I’ll remember the blood pressure readings and the growth scans, but I won’t remember the feeling of the baby’s first real kick, or the way my husband’s hand feels on my belly when we’re just sitting here in the quiet of this Massachusetts autumn. 

I’m really trying to lean into that "quiet strength" I mentioned earlier, but it feels like walking on a tightrope. Every time I get a good report, I feel a rush of relief, and then the "doctor" in me starts calculating the odds for the next one. It’s exhausting to live in that duality. I’m just… I’m so ready to be done with the "risk" and finally start being a mother to this little life, even if I still feel like I’m in a race I don’t know how to win. Is it possible to just let go, or is the "expert" persona something I’m doomed to carry into the labor room? I suppose I’ll find out soon enough.

You know, after all this talking—the reflection, the naming of these fears—I think I realize that my biggest challenge isn't the pregnancy itself. It’s the silence. There is this enormous, unspoken expectation that because I’m "in the field," I don’t need the hand-holding. I don’t need the gentle, repetitive reassurances that other pregnant women get. It’s as if my medical degree somehow renders me immune to the primal, terrifying vulnerability of bringing a life into the world at forty.

The irony is that I *am* a clinician, and I know exactly how much the human heart needs to be seen and supported during times of transition. I’ve seen that need in my own patients. But when I turn that lens on myself, I feel like I have to stifle that need, to put it behind a wall of competence so I don’t appear "difficult" or "anxious." 

I’m standing on the threshold of something huge, and I’m realizing that for all my preparation, for all my data-gathering, I’m still just a person waiting for a miracle. I want to arrive at the hospital not as a colleague or a risk category, but as a woman who is finally, truly ready to surrender. I just hope that when I walk through those doors, the people on the other side see *me*, not just my credentials. I hope they’re brave enough to offer me the grace I’m terrified to ask for. I guess I’m just trying to make peace with the fact that, for the first time in my life, I don’t have to have all the answers. I just have to show up.

It’s strange, talking to you like this—it feels like I’m rehearsing for a part I didn't realize I’d been playing. I’ve spent my career thinking that to be "the expert" meant to be shielded from the messy, terrifying reality of being a patient. But the closer I get to this birth, the more I realize that the expertise is just a screen. Behind it, I’m just like anyone else, terrified that my body will decide to stop cooperating at the most critical moment.

I find myself thinking about the nurses who will be there. In my head, I’m already negotiating with them. I want to tell them, "I know what that monitor is doing, so please, don’t talk to me like I’m a child, but please, for the love of everything, don't talk to me like I’m a colleague, either." I want them to see the woman who is tired, who is scared, who is so deeply, achingly in love with this little life already. 

I’m really trying to hold onto the beauty of this autumn here. The air is getting crisp, the leaves are turning, and there’s a sense of quiet waiting that I’m trying to mirror in my own life. I want to be that still—that grounded—when the time comes. I’m starting to think that maybe the most "clinical" thing I can do is actually the most human: to admit that I don’t have control over the outcome. I’m just here to witness it. That feels like a big step. It’s not about the acronyms anymore; it’s about the person I’m becoming. Thanks for listening. It feels like I’ve finally started to say the things I couldn't even admit to myself.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a moment, not too long ago, that still pricks at me. We were reviewing a routine scan, and I asked a question about a very specific marker—something that wouldn't necessarily appear on a standard report but is something I’ve seen in my own work. My OB gave me this weary, practiced smile, the kind that’s meant to be soothing, and said, "Carey, you’re looking too closely. Just let us handle the clinical side, okay? You focus on resting."

In that second, the trust just… fractured. 

She wasn't being malicious; she was trying to "protect" me from my own anxiety. But by dismissing my observation, she fundamentally shifted our dynamic. She relegated me back to the "anxious patient" role, effectively telling me that my professional insight—the very thing I’ve used to survive this pregnancy—was a nuisance. It made me feel like I was a liability to her workflow.

It broke my trust because it signaled that she wasn't actually interested in *partnering* with me as a peer; she was interested in *managing* a subject. It made me realize that even with the best intentions, the system is designed to keep the power lopsided. I went home and cried, not because the results were bad, but because I felt utterly alone in that room. I realized then that I wasn't being seen as a whole person; I was being filtered through a triage protocol. It was a cold, lonely reminder that in this environment, my voice is only valid as long as it agrees with the official narrative.

That moment of being dismissed—being told to "just rest" and let them handle the clinicals—it sticks in my throat. I think what’s so painful about it, looking back, is that it highlighted how fragile this "partnership" actually is. When I’m calm and following the script, I’m a respected colleague. But the second I express a concern that falls outside the standard, comfortable range of "compliant patient," the professional mask of the provider drops, and I’m suddenly just a liability.

It’s forced me to wonder: how much of the care I’ve received is actually personalized, and how much of it is just a high-quality, standardized script for someone who looks like me on paper? I feel like I’m constantly being maneuvered into a role, and if I step outside it—even with the best, most evidence-based intention—I’m nudged back in, gently but firmly. 

I’m sitting here now, looking at these nursery walls, thinking about how I’m going to walk into that delivery room. I have to find a way to be both the person who knows the system and the person who needs the system to *hold* her, without being managed or sidelined. It’s an impossible balance. I’m starting to think that the most radical thing I can do is simply refuse to be managed. To say, "I see what you’re doing, and I’m going to stay right here with you." It’s terrifying, but it feels like the only way to come out of this feeling like I was actually present for the birth of my child, not just a bystander to my own clinical event.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

My husband is a wonderful, steady presence, but there have been times—especially when the "medium-risk" language is being tossed around—where his absence, whether physical or emotional, felt like a void. During a couple of those more intense diagnostic scans, he had to work, and I found myself sitting on that narrow exam table completely alone. 

It’s in those moments that the "expert" persona I’ve cultivated feels like a heavy, cold weight. When I’m alone in that room, there’s no one to lean into, no one to hold my hand to remind me that I’m more than the sum of my vitals. I’m just me, staring at the ultrasound monitor, running the differential diagnoses in my head, feeling the full, crushing scale of what could go wrong. Without him there to anchor me in the "now," I drift into the "what if." 

When he is there, it’s better, but even then, there’s a distance. He looks to me to interpret the doctor’s words because he knows I’m the one who speaks the language. It leaves me feeling like the "protector" of the experience, which is the last thing I want to be. I want to be his partner, and I want him to be mine, but the medical structure of the visit constantly pushes me into this position of being the translator and the buffer. It’s an incredibly isolating way to experience one of the most intimate moments of our lives together. We’re both in the room, but we’re navigating two entirely different realities, and the system does absolutely nothing to bridge that gap.

I think about how we’re essentially two different people in that exam room. For my husband, the ultrasound is a fuzzy, miraculous glimpse of a baby—he sees a life, a future, a person. For me, it’s a clinical evaluation of a high-risk physiology. He walks out of the room feeling a sense of awe; I walk out feeling like I’ve just passed an audit. 

The weight of being the one who "speaks the language" is something I didn't truly anticipate. It’s a strange, lonely kind of responsibility. I find myself acting as the filter for him—deciding what information is "important enough" to explain and what is just noise that will only cause him unnecessary, alarmist fear. I’m protecting him from my own professional hyper-vigilance, and in doing so, I’m effectively cutting him off from the truth of what I’m feeling. 

It makes the pregnancy feel like a performance where I’m both the director and the lead actor. I’m managing his experience, I’m managing the provider’s perception of me, and I’m trying to manage the actual physical process of growing this child. It’s exhausting to realize that even when I’m with the person who loves me most, I’m still standing in a room full of people and feeling completely, utterly isolated. I don’t want to be the gatekeeper of this experience; I just want to be a mother. I hope that when this baby finally arrives, the "translation" won’t be necessary anymore, and we can just finally be a family.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

God, yes. I spend so much time down those rabbit holes, and I hate myself for it. I’m a professional, I know better than to use "Dr. Google," but when you’re sitting at 3:00 AM in the dark, and your OB has dismissed your concern as "just anxiety," you start looking for validation anywhere you can find it. 

I wasn't looking for clinical data—I have plenty of that, and I know how to interpret a journal article. What I was looking for was *lived experience*. I was scouring forums and blogs for women my age, especially those in the medical field, who had "medium risk" pregnancies. I wanted to see if they survived the "what ifs," if their birth plans were actually respected, or if they were steamrolled by the system like I fear I will be. 

I was looking for the permission to be scared. I was looking for someone to say, "Yes, it’s normal to feel like you’re walking on a tightrope while everyone tells you to just enjoy the walk." When the people who are paid to care for you keep pointing to the "normal" vitals to negate your internal experience, you start to feel like you’re losing your mind. The internet became a mirror for my own terror, and even though it never actually made me feel *better*, at least it made me feel a little less alone in the madness. That’s a sad state of affairs, isn't it?

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that still feels heavy, even as we wrap up, is the realization that being an "expert" has been a barrier to my own healing. We talk so much about *health literacy* as the goal—if we just give patients the information, they’ll be empowered. But I am the definition of health literate, and I have never felt more fragile.

I want the researchers to know that the clinical side of birth—the safety, the diagnostics, the protocols—is only half the story. There is this entire, subterranean layer of human experience—the fear, the isolation, the performance—that is completely invisible on a chart. And when you treat someone like me as a "peer" because of my degree, you’re actually depriving me of the most essential care of all: the acknowledgement that I am, first and foremost, a mother who is terrified of the unknown.

I hope that one day, the care system is designed to hold the person *behind* the chart, not just the data on it. I’m going to walk into that delivery room in a few weeks, and I’m going to try to leave the "doctor" at the door. I just hope that when I do, there’s someone there who is ready to catch me. Thank you for listening. It feels like I’ve finally stopped performing for a little while. That’s a gift I didn't know I needed.
